title: Understanding Deja Vu Phenomenon
overview: This presentation explores the intriguing phenomenon of Deja Vu, examining its nature, scientific theories, and implications for understanding memory and perception.
summary: This presentation explores the intriguing phenomenon of Deja Vu, examining its nature, scientific theories, and implications for understanding memory and perception.

slides.title: What Is Deja Vu?
slides.content:
Deja Vu is a fleeting, uncanny feeling of familiarity.
It feels like you've experienced a situation before, but cannot place it.
This sensation typically happens very briefly and without warning.
It's a mysterious phenomenon that puzzles scientists.
It connects to our understanding of memory and perception.
Our experience of reality is partly constructed by complex processes.

slides.title: Studying Deja Vu Challenges
slides.content:
Capturing Deja Vu in experiments is difficult due to its fleeting nature.
It is a purely subjective experience with no clear physical markers.
Everyone experiences Deja Vu differently, hindering scientific understanding.
There is often no hard, concrete evidence to support reported experiences.
It is difficult to verify these experiences scientifically through self-reporting.
Inducing or controlling Deja Vu in lab settings is not easily achievable.

slides.title: Dual Processing Theory Explained
slides.content:
Brain processes information through fast and slow pathways.
A slight delay in one pathway can trigger Deja Vu.
The brain interprets delayed information as a separate event.
This leads to the feeling of having experienced it before.
It demonstrates how processing speed influences perception.
This helps explain how our perception can feel uncanny.

slides.title: Restaurant Example: Dual Processing
slides.content:
Our senses process information like sights, sounds, and smells in real time.
Sensory information is not always processed instantaneously.
A slight delay occurs while the brain interprets incoming data.
Distraction can cause the brain to replay recent sensory information.
This example highlights dual processing: real-time and replay.
It shows perception is a dynamic, layered process.

slides.title: Hologram Theory of Memory
slides.content:
Memories are stored as fragmented, layered holograms.
A partial cue can trigger the recall of an entire memory.
This explains the sense of familiarity without clear recognition.
Seeing a fragment can evoke the feeling of experiencing the whole.
Small parts of memory can evoke the entire stored memory.
It helps understand fragmented yet familiar memory retrieval.

slides.title: Memory Retrieval and Familiarity [COMPARISON]
slides.content:
Memories are stored as fragmented holograms, each part holding the whole.
A single fragment can trigger the recall of an entire memory.
The brain summons old memories but struggles to identify their source.
This creates a sense of familiarity without explicit recognition.
Seeing a familiar object might evoke a deep, distant memory.
It explains why we remember but can't quite place something.

slides.title: Divided Attention Theory Explained
slides.content:
Deja Vu occurs when your attention is momentarily split.
The brain records details subliminally without conscious awareness.
This happens especially when focus is directed elsewhere.
Peripheral vision details are recorded without noticing.
When attention refocuses, the situation feels oddly familiar.
It shows how the mind processes information subliminally.

slides.title: Key Takeaways on Deja Vu
slides.content:
Deja Vu is a complex interplay of memory and perception.
Multiple theories like dual processing, hologram, and divided attention exist.
Studying Deja Vu is challenging due to its subjective nature.
Paying attention to personal experiences can offer insights.
Understanding Deja Vu sheds light on human consciousness.
Further exploration promises insights into the human mind.
